questions.question: The Inca Empire, conquered by Pizarro in the 1530s, had its capital in which city?
questions.explanation: Cusco (in modern Peru) was the capital of the Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu — 'the four regions'). Machu Picchu, built around 1450, was not the capital but a royal estate and religious site. The Inca built an extensive road system (~40,000 km), performed sophisticated surgery, developed advanced agricultural terracing, and ran a complex administrative system — all without a writing system. Cusco was described by Spanish conquistadors as more magnificent than any city in Europe.
questions.memory_hook: Cusco was the Inca capital — Spanish conquistadors said it was more magnificent than any European city. The Incas had no writing but administered 12 million people across 40,000 km of roads.

questions.question: The US stock market crash of October 1929, often called 'Black Tuesday,' triggered what global event?
questions.explanation: The Wall Street Crash of 1929 (October 24-29) wiped out billions in investor wealth. Banks failed, businesses closed, and unemployment in the US reached 25% by 1933. The Depression spread globally as international trade collapsed. Franklin Roosevelt's New Deal programs (1933-39) provided relief but full economic recovery only came with WWII military production. The Depression fundamentally changed attitudes toward government's role in the economy.
questions.memory_hook: The 1929 crash wiped out paper fortunes — but the real damage was the bank failures that destroyed real savings. US unemployment hit 25% by 1933, and only WWII ended the Depression.

questions.question: Ho Chi Minh led the Viet Minh independence movement against which colonial power before fighting the US in Vietnam?
questions.explanation: France colonized Vietnam, Cambodia, and Laos as 'French Indochina' from the 1880s. Ho Chi Minh founded the Viet Minh in 1941 to fight both Japanese occupation and French colonialism. After WWII, France attempted to reassert control; Ho Chi Minh's forces defeated them at the Battle of Dien Bien Phu (1954). Vietnam was temporarily divided at the 17th parallel, setting up the conditions for US involvement that began in earnest in the early 1960s.
questions.memory_hook: Ho Chi Minh first fought French colonialism — defeating France at Dien Bien Phu in 1954 — then fought American intervention. He said America was just the French with more money.

questions.question: Jim Thorpe, an Indigenous American athlete of Sac and Fox ancestry, won the pentathlon and decathlon at the 1912 Stockholm Olympics. What happened to his medals?
options: He sold them for money during the Depression
They were stripped because he had played minor-league baseball for pay, then restored posthumously in 1982
He donated them to his tribe
He lost them in a house fire
questions.answer: They were stripped because he had played minor-league baseball for pay, then restored posthumously in 1982
questions.explanation: Jim Thorpe was declared a professional and stripped of his 1912 Olympic medals because he had been paid $2/day to play minor-league baseball in 1909-10. By modern rules this would not be a violation, and the IOC restored his medals posthumously in 1982. Thorpe is also considered one of the greatest American football players ever, playing professionally until age 41. King Gustav V of Sweden called him 'the greatest athlete in the world.'
questions.memory_hook: Thorpe won two Olympic golds in 1912 — then was stripped for playing semi-pro baseball for $2 a day two years earlier. They were only restored 70 years later, after his death. King Gustaf called him 'the greatest athlete in the world.'

questions.question: Wimbledon, the oldest Grand Slam tennis tournament, has been played at the All England Club since 1877. What unique surface does it use?
questions.explanation: Wimbledon is played on natural grass, making it unique among the four Grand Slams: Australian Open (hard court), French Open (clay), Wimbledon (grass), US Open (hard court). The grass favors low bounces and faster play, historically benefiting serve-and-volley players. The balls must be kept at 68°F (20°C). Wimbledon has strict all-white dress code for players and is famous for strawberries and cream consumed by spectators.
questions.memory_hook: Wimbledon's grass is the last major played on it — making the ball skid low and fast, favoring big servers. The all-white dress code and strawberries-and-cream tradition remain strictly maintained.

questions.question: 'Toy Story' (1995) was the first feature film created entirely using computer-generated imagery (CGI). Which studio produced it?
questions.explanation: Pixar Animation Studios produced Toy Story in collaboration with Walt Disney Pictures. It was the first full-length computer-animated feature film, a monumental technical achievement. The film grossed $362 million globally on a $30 million budget and launched a franchise that includes 4 films and counting. The voice cast included Tom Hanks (Woody) and Tim Allen (Buzz Lightyear). Pixar had previously created only short films and computer-animated TV commercials.
questions.memory_hook: Toy Story (1995) was the first all-CGI feature film — Pixar had only made shorts before. The technology was so new they couldn't animate convincing human faces, which is why all the human characters are minor.

questions.question: Prince's 1984 album and film 'Purple Rain' was set in which US city?
questions.explanation: Purple Rain is set in Minneapolis, Minnesota — Prince's hometown. He worked and recorded at his Paisley Park estate and studios in nearby Chanhassen. Minneapolis has become closely associated with Prince's legacy since his death in 2016. The 'Minneapolis Sound' — a funk, rock, and R&B fusion — was Prince's sonic signature. The film portrays the Minneapolis music scene in the early 1980s and features the First Avenue nightclub, a real Minneapolis venue.
questions.memory_hook: Prince put Minneapolis on the music map — far from LA or NYC, he built an empire at Paisley Park. First Avenue club, where Purple Rain was filmed, remains a Minneapolis landmark.

questions.question: Which author wrote the novel 'The Catcher in the Rye' (1951), narrated by the rebellious teenager Holden Caulfield?
questions.explanation: J.D. Salinger published The Catcher in the Rye in 1951, narrated by Holden Caulfield — a teenage boy expelled from boarding school who wanders New York City for a weekend. The novel's themes of alienation, phoniness, and adolescent rebellion made it enormously influential. Salinger became famously reclusive, refusing interviews and literary publicity after its success. The novel has been consistently challenged and banned in schools.
questions.memory_hook: Holden Caulfield's contempt for 'phonies' made Catcher in the Rye the defining novel of adolescent alienation. Salinger found fame so intolerable he retreated to a New Hampshire compound and stopped publishing for decades.

questions.question: The Indochina Peninsula's major rivers — the Irrawaddy, Salween, Mekong, and Red River — all originate in which region?
questions.explanation: The Tibetan Plateau (the 'roof of the world') is the source of most of Southeast and South Asia's major rivers: the Mekong, Salween, Irrawaddy, Yangtze, Yellow, Brahmaputra, Indus, and Ganges all originate there. The plateau acts as Asia's 'water tower,' with its glaciers providing seasonal flow to rivers that support over 3 billion people. Climate change-induced glacier retreat threatens long-term water security for vast portions of Asia.
questions.memory_hook: The Tibetan Plateau is Asia's 'water tower' — source of 10 major rivers serving 3 billion people. Tibetan glacier loss from climate change threatens water security on a continental scale.

questions.question: The Rocky Mountains contain the Continental Divide. What happens to precipitation falling on either side of this divide?
options: East-side rain flows to the Pacific; west-side flows to the Gulf of Mexico
East-side flows to the Atlantic/Gulf of Mexico; west-side flows to the Pacific
All precipitation flows into the Great Lakes
questions.answer: East-side flows to the Atlantic/Gulf of Mexico; west-side flows to the Pacific
questions.explanation: The Continental Divide (also called the Great Divide) follows the crest of the Rocky Mountains. Rain or snow falling east of the divide eventually reaches the Atlantic Ocean or Gulf of Mexico (via the Missouri, Mississippi, Arkansas, or Rio Grande rivers). Water falling west of the divide flows to the Pacific (via the Columbia, Colorado, or other rivers). The divide creates one of North America's fundamental geographic patterns.
questions.memory_hook: The Continental Divide is where a raindrop chooses its ocean — east goes to the Atlantic, west to the Pacific. The same ridge separates river systems that drain half a continent each.

questions.question: What is the greenhouse effect, and why does it make Earth habitable in its natural form?
options: It traps pollution in lower atmosphere layers, creating smog
Greenhouse gases (CO₂, water vapor, methane) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, warming Earth's surface to temperatures that support life
It increases oxygen levels in the stratosphere
It reflects UV radiation, protecting life from solar damage
questions.answer: Greenhouse gases (CO₂, water vapor, methane) absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, warming Earth's surface to temperatures that support life
questions.explanation: Without any greenhouse effect, Earth's average temperature would be about -18°C (0°F) — too cold for liquid water and life. Greenhouse gases absorb outgoing infrared radiation and redirect some back to Earth's surface, maintaining an average of +15°C. The problem isn't the greenhouse effect itself but the enhanced greenhouse effect from human-added CO₂ and methane, which is pushing temperatures above historical ranges.
questions.memory_hook: Without greenhouse gases, Earth would be -18°C — a frozen rock. The natural greenhouse effect keeps us at +15°C. Human emissions are adding extra insulation, pushing temperatures higher than the range life adapted to.

questions.question: Convergent evolution creates similar structures in unrelated species. What is the corresponding term for structures that look different but evolved from the same ancestral structure (like human arms and whale fins)?
questions.explanation: Homologous structures share common ancestry but may have very different functions. Human hands, cat paws, bat wings, whale fins, and bird wings all have the same underlying bone structure (humerus, radius/ulna, carpals, metacarpals, phalanges) because they all evolved from the same ancestral limb. This bone arrangement reveals evolutionary relationships even when the functions differ radically. Analogous structures (like bird wings and butterfly wings) serve similar functions but come from different origins.
questions.memory_hook: Human arms and whale fins have the same bones (humerus, radius, ulna, carpals) from the same ancestor — just reshaped by evolution. Same origin, different function = homologous. Compare to analogous: same function, different origin.
